neutral.summary: State legislators are divided over a proposal to increase education funding by $2 billion, with debates focusing on property tax implications.

left.take: Investing in our kids is investing in our future. The wealthy can afford to pay their fair share so every child gets a quality education.

quotes.text: We cannot continue to underfund our schools while giving tax breaks to corporations.
quotes.speaker: Rep. Lisa Thompson

quotes.text: Every child deserves access to art, music, sports, and small class sizes. That costs money.
quotes.speaker: Teachers Union President Marcus Williams

quotes.text: Our schools are crumbling while billionaires get richer. Time to change priorities.
quotes.speaker: Education Advocate Sandra Lee

quotes.text: States that invest in education see better economic outcomes. This is about our future competitiveness.
quotes.speaker: Education Policy Prof. Dr. James Mitchell

quotes.text: Teachers are leaving the profession because we don't pay them enough. Funding matters.
quotes.speaker: School Principal Angela Rodriguez

right.take: More money doesn't automatically mean better schools. We need accountability and school choice, not higher taxes on homeowners.

quotes.text: Parents, not bureaucrats, should decide where their children go to school.
quotes.speaker: Sen. Robert Hayes

quotes.text: We've thrown money at this problem for decades. Time to try accountability and competition.
quotes.speaker: Education Reform Activist David Chen

quotes.text: School choice and charter schools are delivering better results at lower cost.
quotes.speaker: Charter School Director Jennifer Martinez

quotes.text: Property taxes are already crushing families. We can't keep raising them.
quotes.speaker: Taxpayer Association President Mike Anderson
